title: In-situ correlative SEM/KPFM for semiconductor devices and 2D heterostructures
authors: Prabhu Prasad Swain, Nahid Hosseini, Eveline. S Mayner, Aleksandra Radenovic, Marcos Penedo, Georg E. Fantner
abstract: Correlative nanoscale surface characterization benefits from simultaneously measuring electronic and structural properties in the same environment, a capability that is essential for modern-day materials science and semiconductor failure analysis. In-situ AFM-SEM measurements facilitated by self-sensing cantilevers offer great potential here; however, they are limited due to their inherent capacitive crosstalk. Here, we demonstrate for the first time the in-situ implementation of single-pass heterodyne Kelvin probe force microscopy inside a scanning electron microscope, using piezo-resistive cantilevers. We overcome the capacitive crosstalk prevalent in piezo-resistive cantilevers by demodulating excitation and detection to simultaneously map surface topography and contact potential difference for correlation with compositional analysis. We systematically compare different operational modes of this heterodyne technique, elucidating their spatial resolution, signal sensitivity, and signal-to-noise ratio. The integrated approach yields exceptional signal quality and reveals how electron beam scan parameters can directly influence surface potential contrast. We demonstrate this correlative analysis workflow on two-dimensional heterostructures and semiconductor circuits. This work establishes a robust and versatile correlative imaging mode for in-situ Kelvin force and topography imaging inside a scanning electron microscope for next-generation semiconductor device analysis and materials science.
summary: This paper introduces a novel, robust technique for performing in-situ Kelvin Probe Force Microscopy (KPFM) inside a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). By employing single-pass heterodyne detection, the authors overcome capacitive crosstalk to simultaneously map surface topography and local electronic potential (CPD) on semiconductor devices and 2D heterostructures. This allows for unprecedented correlative analysis crucial for failure analysis and fundamental materials characterization.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving simultaneous, in-situ measurement of electronic and structural properties in advanced semiconductor and 2D heterostructure devices.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated a robust, versatile correlative imaging mode by implementing single-pass heterodyne KPFM inside an SEM, overcoming capacitive crosstalk.
method: They utilized a single-pass heterodyne Kelvin probe force microscopy technique, demodulating excitation and detection signals to map topography and Contact Potential Difference (CPD) simultaneously.
model_or_system: The work focuses on semiconductor circuits and 2D heterostructures (like hBN on ITO) analyzed using a self-sensing, piezoresistive cantilever setup integrated within an SEM.
key_observables: Topography, Contact Potential Difference (CPD), and elemental composition (via EDS).
important_parameters: CPD energy resolution of 1–20 meV; spatial resolution <10 nm; operation in vacuum environment.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that heterodyne detection effectively decouples excitation and detection signals, mitigating capacitive crosstalk inherent to self-sensing cantilevers.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the setup integration, the principle of heterodyne KPFM, and comparative maps showing correlated SEM/KPFM/Topography data on both semiconductor circuits and 2D heterostructures, including effects of electron beam exposure.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the need for correlative measurements, detailing the novel heterodyne KPFM technique to overcome crosstalk, demonstrating its application on 2D materials and circuits, and finally analyzing the complex influence of electron and ion beam exposure on the measured surface potential.
why_interesting: While highly experimental, the detailed analysis of surface potential shifts due to beam exposure (charge trapping, contamination) provides insights into surface physics and charge dynamics in nanoscale devices, which can inform models of open quantum systems interacting with noisy environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
